does: This paper introduces AutoRed, a framework for automatically generating adversarial prompts to test LLM safety without relying on seed instructions. The method operates in two stages: first generating diverse adversarial instructions through persona-guided generation, then iteratively refining low-quality prompts via a reflection loop. A novel verifier component assesses prompt harmfulness without querying target models, improving computational efficiency. The authors construct two red teaming datasets (AutoRed-Medium and AutoRed-Hard) and evaluate eight state-of-the-art LLMs, demonstrating that AutoRed achieves higher attack success rates and better generalization across models compared to existing seed-based red teaming approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing field of LLM safety evaluation and adversarial testing, extending beyond earlier red teaming methods that depend on manually curated or sampled seed prompts to guide attack generation. It responds to limitations in semantic diversity observed in seed-based approaches by removing this dependency entirely. The framework combines ideas from automated prompt optimization, persona-guided generation, and iterative refinement techniques that have emerged in recent LLM work. The inclusion of a verifier without target model querying relates to broader research on efficient safety assessment and surrogate models for evaluation, addressing computational costs that plague large-scale red teaming campaigns.
enables: By demonstrating free-form adversarial prompt generation without seeds, AutoRed opens pathways for more scalable and semantically diverse safety evaluation of LLMs. The released datasets provide standardized benchmarks for comparing future red teaming methods and safety improvements. The verifier mechanism could be extended to develop more efficient safety assessment tools that avoid expensive queries to large models. This work supports the development of more robust defenses by exposing diverse failure modes, and may inspire follow-up research into automated prompt generation for other safety-critical applications. The framework's architecture suggests directions for combining language model creativity with systematic vulnerability discovery.
